title: Charmed Life - Chapter 13 (Fragment 1)
text:
Chapter 13

13

T
 HEY WERE USHERED into a private parlor in the White Hart. Mr. Henry Nostrum rolled pompously to meet them.

“My dear young friends!” He put his hands on Janet’s shoulders and kissed her. Janet started backwards, knocking her hat over one ear. Cat was a little shaken. He had forgotten Mr. Nostrum’s seedy, shabby look, and the weird effect of his wandering left eye. “Sit down, sit down!” said Mr. Nostrum heartily. “Have some ginger beer.”

They sat down. They sipped ginger beer, which neither of them liked. “What did you want me for, as well as Gwendolen?” Cat asked.

“Because,” said Mr. Nostrum, “to come straight to the point and not to beat about the bush, we find, as we rather feared we would, that we are quite unable to make use of those three signatures which you were kind enough to donate to me for services rendered in the tuition line. The Person Who Inhabits That Castle Yonder, whose name I disdain to say, signs his name under unbreakable protections. You may call it prudent of him. But I fear it necessitates our using Plan Two. Which was why, my dear Cat, we were so glad to arrange for you to live at the Castle.”

“What is Plan Two?” said Janet.

Mr. Nostrum’s odd eye slipped sideways across Janet’s face. He did not seem to realize she was not Gwendolen. Perhaps his wandering eye did not see very well. “Plan Two is just as I described it to you, my dear Gwendolen,” he said. “We have not changed it one whit.”

Janet had to try another way to find out what he was talking about. She was getting quite good at it. “I want you to describe it to Cat, though,” she said. “He doesn’t know about it, and he may need to because—because most unfortunately they’ve taken my witchcraft away.”

Mr. Nostrum wagged a playful finger at her. “Yes, naughty girl. I’ve been hearing things about you in the village. A sad thing to lose, but let us hope it will only be temporary. Now—as to explaining to Young Chant—how shall I best go about it?” He thought, smoothing his frizzy wings of hair, as his habit was. Somehow, the way he did it showed Cat that whatever Mr. Nostrum was going to tell him, it would not be quite the truth. It was in the movement of Mr. Nostrum’s hands, and in the very sit of his silver watch-chain across his shabby, rounded waistcoat.

“Well, Young Chant,” said Mr. Nostrum, “this is the matter in a nutshell. There is a group, a clique, a collection of people, headed by the Master of the Castle, who are behaving very selfishly in connection with witchcraft. They are keeping all the best things to themselves, which of course makes them very dangerous—a threat to all witches, and a looming disaster to ordinary people. For instance, take dragons’ blood. You know that it is banned. These people, with That Person at their head, had it banned, and yet—mark this well, Young Chant—they use it daily themselves. And—here is my point—they keep tight control of the ways to get to the worlds where dragons’ blood comes from. An ordinary necromancer like myself can only get it at great risk and expense, and our exotic suppliers have to endanger themselves to get it for us. And the same goes for almost any product from another world.

“Now, I ask you, Young Chant, is this fair? No. And I’ll tell you why not, young Eric. It is not fair that the ways to other worlds should be in the hands of a few. That is the crux of the matter: the ways to other worlds. We want them opened up, made free to everyone. And that is where you come in, Young Chant. The best and easiest way, the broadest Gateway to Elsewhere, if I may put it like that, is a certain enclosed garden in the grounds of this said Castle. I expect you have been forbidden to enter it—”

vocabulary.definition: Officially or legally prohibited.
vocabulary.example: You know that it is banned.

vocabulary.definition: A small exclusive group of people.
vocabulary.example: There is a group, a clique, a collection of people.

vocabulary.definition: The most important or decisive point.
vocabulary.example: That is the crux of the matter.

vocabulary.definition: The feeling that someone or something is unworthy of respect.
vocabulary.example: Whose name I disdain to say.

vocabulary.definition: Suppliers that are unusual or foreign, often rare or highly sought after.
vocabulary.example: Our exotic suppliers have to endanger themselves to get it for us.

vocabulary.definition: Not allowed to go into a place.
vocabulary.example: You have been forbidden to enter it.

vocabulary.definition: Characterised by tightly curled hair.
vocabulary.example: He thought, smoothing his frizzy wings of hair.

vocabulary.definition: A figurative or literal entrance to another place or realm.
vocabulary.example: The broadest Gateway to Elsewhere.

vocabulary.definition: To receive information or rumors about a person.
vocabulary.example: I’ve been hearing things about you in the village.

vocabulary.definition: An impending or threatening catastrophe.
vocabulary.example: A looming disaster to ordinary people.

vocabulary.definition: Saying secrets or talking too much (idiomatic, context inferred).
vocabulary.example: I’ve been hearing things about you in the village.

vocabulary.definition: Pay close attention to this point.
vocabulary.example: Mark this well, Young Chant.

vocabulary.definition: Makes something necessary.
vocabulary.example: I fear it necessitates our using Plan Two.

vocabulary.definition: Affectedly grand or self-important.
vocabulary.example: Mr. Henry Nostrum rolled pompously to meet them.

vocabulary.definition: In a way that is grand, self-important, or overly formal.
vocabulary.example: Mr. Henry Nostrum rolled pompously to meet them.

vocabulary.definition: To greet someone in a grand or self-important manner.
vocabulary.example: Mr. Henry Nostrum rolled pompously to meet them.

vocabulary.definition: Given or provided (usually services).
vocabulary.example: Services rendered in the tuition line.

vocabulary.definition: Looking disreputable or shabby.
vocabulary.example: Mr. Nostrum’s seedy, shabby look.

vocabulary.definition: In a way that shows concern only for oneself.
vocabulary.example: Behaving very selfishly in connection with witchcraft.

vocabulary.definition: In poor condition through long use or lack of care.
vocabulary.example: Mr. Nostrum’s seedy, shabby look.

vocabulary.definition: Strict or firm regulation or management.
vocabulary.example: They keep tight control of the ways to get to the worlds.

vocabulary.definition: To avoid talking about what is important.
vocabulary.example: To come straight to the point and not to beat about the bush.

vocabulary.definition: The field or area related to teaching or tutoring.
vocabulary.example: Services rendered in the tuition line.

vocabulary.definition: Strong safeguards that cannot be bypassed or broken.
vocabulary.example: His name signs under unbreakable protections.

vocabulary.definition: To lead or escort someone to a place.
vocabulary.example: They were ushered into a private parlor in the White Hart.

vocabulary.definition: To shake or wave one's finger, often playfully or as a warning.
vocabulary.example: Mr. Nostrum wagged a playful finger at her.

vocabulary.definition: An eye that moves or looks around uncontrollably or is misaligned.
vocabulary.example: The weird effect of his wandering left eye.
